corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: 17 Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2019 Anaphora/Coreference Coreference refers to when multiple expressions refer to the same entity or event.

context_window.previous: For example, the premise “Putin is so entrenched within Russias ruling system that many of its members can imagine no other leader” entails “Putin is so entrenched within Russias ruling system that many of its members can imagine no other leader than Putin” and contradicts “Putin is so entrenched within Russias ruling system that many of its members can imagine no other leader than themselves.” This is often regarded as a special case of anaphora, but we decided to split out these cases from explicit anaphora, which is often also regarded as a case of coreference (and attempted to some degree in modern coreference resolution systems).

location.sentence: Alternations: Active/Passive, Genitives/Partitives, Nominalization, Datives All four of these categories correspond to syntactic alternations that are known to follow specific patterns in English: • Active/Passive: “I saw him” is equivalent to “He was seen by me” and entails “He was seen”. • Genitives/Partitives: “the elephant’s foot” is the same thing as “the foot of the elephant”. • Nominalization: “I caused him to submit his resignation” entails “I caused the submission of his resignation”. • Datives: “I baked him a cake” entails “I baked a cake for him” and “I baked a cake” but not “I baked him”.

context_window.previous: Other rearrangements of core arguments, such as those seen in Symmetry/Collectivity, also fall under the Core Arguments label.

location.sentence: In all of these examples, “pet squirrel” appears in what we call the restrictor position of the quantifier.

context_window.previous: We can demon- strate how the quantifiers “a”, “no” and “exactly one” differ with respect to monotonicity: • “I have a pet squirrel” entails “I have a pet”, but not “I have a happy pet squirrel”. • “I have no pet squirrels” does not entail “I have no pets”, but does entail “I have no happy pet squirrels”. • “I have exactly one pet squirrel” entails neither “I have exactly one pet” nor “I have exactly one happy pet squirrel”.
context_window.current: In all of these examples, “pet squirrel” appears in what we call the restrictor position of the quantifier.
context_window.next: We say: • “a” is upward monotone in its restrictor: an entailment in the restrictor yields an entailment of the whole statement. • “no” is downward monotone in its restrictor: an entailment in the restrictor yields an en- tailment of the whole statement in the opposite direction. • “exactly one” is non-monotone in its restrictor: entailments in the restrictor do not yield entailments of the whole statement.

location.sentence: Propositional Structure: Negation, Double Negation, Conjunction, Disjunction, Conditionals All of the basic operations of propositional logic appear in natural language, and we tag them where they are relevant to our examples: • Negation: “The cat sat on the mat” contradicts “The cat did not sit on the mat”. • Double negation: “The market is not impossible to navigate” entails “The market is possi- ble to navigate”.

context_window.previous: The notion of entailment is also borrowed from mathematical logic.

top_candidates.sentence: Relative to the models trained directly on the GLUE tasks, InferSent is competitive and GenSen outperforms all but the two best.
On WNLI, no model exceeds most-frequent-class guessing (65.1%) and we substitute the model predictions for the most-frequent baseline.
The models trained on the GLUE tasks largely outperform the pretrained sentence representation models, with the exception of GenSen.
When evaluating these models on our diagnostic dataset, we find that they fail (often spectacularly) on many linguistic phenomena, suggesting possible avenues for future work.
In sum, the question of how to design general-purpose NLU models remains unanswered, and we believe that GLUE can provide fertile soil for addressing this challenge.
We use the original pre-trained model trained on sequences of sentences from the Toronto Book Corpus (Zhu et al.
GenSen , a sequence-to-sequence model trained on a variety of supervised and unsupervised objectives.
We use the variant of the model trained on both MNLI and SNLI, the Skip-Thought objective on TBC, and a constituency parsing objective on the Billion Word Benchmark.
The GLUE benchmark follows the same evaluation model as SemEval and Kaggle.
To evaluate a system on the benchmark, one must run the system on the provided test data for the tasks, then upload the results to the website for scoring.
The benchmark site shows per-task scores and a macro-average of those scores to determine a system's position on the leaderboard.
For tasks with multiple metrics (e.g., accuracy and F1), we use an unweighted average of the metrics as the score for the task when computing the overall macro-average.
For tasks with multiple metrics (e.g., accuracy and F1), we use an unweighted average of the metrics as the score for the task when computing the overall macro-average.

While the main benchmark mostly reflects an application-driven distribution of examples, our diagnostic dataset highlights a pre-defined set of phenomena that we believe are interesting and important for models to capture.

While the main benchmark mostly reflects an application-driven distribution of examples, our diagnostic dataset highlights a pre-defined set of phenomena that we believe are interesting and important for models to capture.
Unlike on the main benchmark, the multi-task models are almost always outperformed by their single-task counterparts.

We expect that our platform and diagnostic dataset will be useful for similar analyses in the future, so that model designers can better understand their models' generalization behavior and implicit knowledge.
InferSent , a BiLSTM with max-pooling trained on MNLI and SNLI.
The website also provides fine-and coarse-grained results on the diagnostic dataset.
A sample from our dataset is shown in Table .
We present performance on the main benchmark tasks in Table .
For natural language understanding (NLU) technology to be maximally useful, it must be able to process language in a way that is not exclusive to a single task, genre, or dataset.

In pursuit of this objective, we introduce the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark, a collection of tools for evaluating the performance of models across a diverse set of existing NLU tasks.

In pursuit of this objective, we introduce the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark, a collection of tools for evaluating the performance of models across a diverse set of existing NLU tasks.

To facilitate research in this direction, we present the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark: a collection of NLU tasks including question answering, sentiment analysis, and textual entailment, and an associated online platform for model evaluation, comparison, and analysis.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
